reasoning: The Federal Reserve engaged in steady rate cuts between October 2025 and February 2026. Data from the FRED tool shows the EFFR at 4.11 in October, 3.88 in mid-November, and 3.64 from December onward. The November rate cut took place before or around November 9, meaning the baseline rate on that date was either 3.88 (if the cut had taken effect) or 4.11 (if it hadn't). For the first resolution date (Nov 16), the rate had stabilized at 3.88 (or dropped to it), representing no increase. For the second date (Dec 9), the rate was still 3.88 before the December FOMC meeting. For the final date (Feb 7), the rate had been cut significantly to 3.64. Therefore, the absolute probability of the EFFR being strictly higher on any of the target dates compared to Nov 9 is almost definitively negligible, mostly bounded to extremely rare 1-2bp daily upward noise which hasn't historically disturbed a persistent downtrend. Following the explicit instructions to increase uncertainty for distant horizons, I have spaced probabilities slightly up toward 0.5.
